questions.stem: The determinant of the $2 \times 2$ matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$ is which expression?
questions.explanation: For a $2 \times 2$ matrix, $\det A = ad - bc$: the product of the main diagonal minus the product of the off-diagonal. A nonzero determinant means the matrix is invertible.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: What is the determinant of $\begin{pmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 3 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$?
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: A square system $Ax = d$ has a unique solution if and only if which condition holds?
questions.explanation: A unique solution exists precisely when $A$ is nonsingular, that is $\det A \neq 0$. Then $A^{-1}$ exists and $x = A^{-1}d$. A zero determinant means no unique solution.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: Using Cramer's rule on the system $2x + 3y = 12$, $x - y = 1$, the determinant of the coefficient matrix is which value?
questions.explanation: The coefficient matrix is $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 3 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$, with $\det = (2)(-1) - (3)(1) = -2 - 3 = -5$.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: For the system $2x + 3y = 12$, $x - y = 1$, Cramer's rule gives the solution for $x$ as which value?
questions.explanation: Replace the first column with the right-hand side: $\det A_x = \begin{vmatrix} 12 & 3 \\ 1 & -1 \end{vmatrix} = -12 - 3 = -15$. Then $x = \det A_x / \det A = -15 / -5 = 3$.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: For the system $2x + 3y = 12$, $x - y = 1$, the value of $y$ is which of the following?
questions.explanation: Replace the second column: $\det A_y = \begin{vmatrix} 2 & 12 \\ 1 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = 2 - 12 = -10$. Then $y = -10 / -5 = 2$. Check: $2(3) + 3(2) = 12$ and $3 - 2 = 1$.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: The inverse of $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$ is which matrix?
questions.explanation: Here $\det A = (2)(4) - (1)(3) = 5$. The inverse of a $2 \times 2$ matrix is $\dfrac{1}{\det A}\begin{pmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{pmatrix} = \dfrac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 4 & -1 \\ -3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: In the Leontief input-output model, the system that determines gross output $x$ from final demand $d$ is which equation?
questions.explanation: Gross output must cover both interindustry use $Ax$ and final demand $d$: $x = Ax + d$. Rearranging gives $(I - A)x = d$, so $x = (I - A)^{-1} d$ when the Leontief inverse exists.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: In the Leontief model, the entry $a_{ij}$ of the technology matrix $A$ represents which quantity?
options: The amount of input $i$ needed per unit of output $j$.
questions.explanation: The technical coefficient $a_{ij}$ is the amount of sector $i$'s output used as an input to produce one unit of sector $j$'s output. The column $j$ lists all inputs needed per unit of good $j$.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: A two-sector economy has technology matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} 0.2 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.1 \end{pmatrix}$ and final demand $d = \begin{pmatrix} 10 \\ 10 \end{pmatrix}$. The matrix $I - A$ is which of the following?
questions.explanation: Subtract $A$ from the identity: $I - A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 - 0.2 & -0.3 \\ -0.4 & 1 - 0.1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.8 & -0.3 \\ -0.4 & 0.9 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: For the Leontief system with $I - A = \begin{pmatrix} 0.8 & -0.3 \\ -0.4 & 0.9 \end{pmatrix}$, the determinant of $I - A$ is which value?
questions.explanation: $\det(I - A) = (0.8)(0.9) - (-0.3)(-0.4) = 0.72 - 0.12 = 0.6$. A positive determinant here lets us invert and solve for output.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: Solving $(I - A)x = d$ with $I - A = \begin{pmatrix} 0.8 & -0.3 \\ -0.4 & 0.9 \end{pmatrix}$ and $d = \begin{pmatrix} 10 \\ 10 \end{pmatrix}$, the gross output of sector 1, $x_1$, is which value?
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: For the same Leontief system, the gross output of sector 2, $x_2$, is which value?
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: Matrix multiplication $AB$ is defined only when which dimensional condition holds?
options: The number of columns of $A$ equals the number of rows of $B$.
questions.explanation: For $AB$ to exist, the inner dimensions must match: if $A$ is $m \times n$ then $B$ must be $n \times p$, giving a product of size $m \times p$. The number of columns of $A$ must equal the number of rows of $B$.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: If $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$ and $B = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 1 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$, the product $AB$ is which matrix?
questions.explanation: Row by column: top-left $= 1(2) + 2(1) = 4$; top-right $= 1(0) + 2(4) = 8$; bottom-left $= 0(2) + 3(1) = 3$; bottom-right $= 0(0) + 3(4) = 12$. So $AB = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 8 \\ 3 & 12 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: The determinant of the upper-triangular matrix $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & 4 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ is which value?
questions.explanation: The determinant of a triangular matrix is the product of its diagonal entries: $1 \times 1 \times 2 = 2$.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: A linear market model reduces to the triangular system $5P_1 = 100$ and $2P_1 + 4P_2 = 60$. Solving by back-substitution, $P_2$ equals which value?
questions.explanation: From the first equation $P_1 = 20$. Substitute into the second: $2(20) + 4P_2 = 60$, so $40 + 4P_2 = 60$, giving $4P_2 = 20$ and $P_2 = 5$.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: If $\det(I - A) = 0$ in a Leontief model, what does this signify economically?
options: The economy can meet any final demand uniquely.
No unique non-negative output vector solves the system; the technology is not viable.
questions.explanation: A zero determinant means $I - A$ is singular, so $(I - A)^{-1}$ does not exist and the system $(I - A)x = d$ has no unique solution. Economically the technology fails the viability (Hawkins-Simon) requirement.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: For an invertible matrix $A$, the product $A A^{-1}$ equals which matrix?
questions.explanation: By definition of the inverse, $A A^{-1} = A^{-1} A = I$, the identity matrix. This is exactly why $x = A^{-1} d$ solves $Ax = d$: premultiplying by $A^{-1}$ leaves $Ix = x$.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: Solve the system $x + y + z = 6$, $y + 4z = 12$, $2z = 4$ by back-substitution. The value of $x$ is which of the following?
questions.explanation: From the last equation $z = 2$. Then $y + 4(2) = 12$ gives $y = 4$. Finally $x + 4 + 2 = 6$ gives $x = 6 - 4 - 2 = 0$.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: Cramer's rule expresses the solution for variable $x_j$ in $Ax = d$ as which ratio?
questions.explanation: Cramer's rule gives $x_j = \det A_j / \det A$, where $A_j$ is $A$ with its $j$th column replaced by the right-hand-side vector $d$. It requires $\det A \neq 0$.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: A two-good market gives the linear system $4P_1 - P_2 = 10$, $-2P_1 + 5P_2 = 8$. Using Cramer's rule, the determinant of the coefficient matrix is which value?
questions.explanation: The coefficient matrix is $\begin{pmatrix} 4 & -1 \\ -2 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$, with $\det = (4)(5) - (-1)(-2) = 20 - 2 = 18$.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: Solve the system $3x + 2y = 16$, $x + 4y = 12$ by Cramer's rule. Give the coefficient determinant, then $x$ and $y$.
questions.solution: The coefficient determinant is $\det A = (3)(4) - (2)(1) =$ {{1}}. Then $x = \det A_x / \det A$ with $\det A_x = (16)(4) - (2)(12) = 40$, so $x =$ {{2}}. And $y = \det A_y / \det A$ with $\det A_y = (3)(12) - (16)(1) = 20$, so $y =$ {{3}}.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: A two-sector Leontief economy has $A = \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 & 0.4 \\ 0.5 & 0.2 \end{pmatrix}$ and final demand $d = \begin{pmatrix} 18 \\ 9 \end{pmatrix}$. Find $\det(I - A)$ and the gross output of sector 1.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: Find the inverse of $A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 5 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$. Give its determinant, then state whether the matrix is invertible.
questions.solution: The determinant is $\det A = (3)(2) - (1)(5) =$ {{1}}. Since this is nonzero, the matrix {{2}} invertible, and $A^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -5 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.explanation: $\det A = 6 - 5 = 1$. A nonzero determinant means the matrix is invertible. With $\det A = 1$, $A^{-1} = \frac{1}{1}\begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -5 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: Solve $5x + 2y = 24$, $3x - y = 10$ by Cramer's rule. Give the coefficient determinant and the values of $x$ and $y$.
questions.solution: The coefficient determinant is $\det A = (5)(-1) - (2)(3) =$ {{1}}. Then $x =$ {{2}} and $y =$ {{3}}.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: Compute the determinant of $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 3 & 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 0 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$ by expanding along the second column, and state whether the system $Ax = d$ has a unique solution.
questions.solution: Expanding along the second column (which has a single nonzero entry $1$ in row 2), $\det A = 1 \cdot \begin{vmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 4 \end{vmatrix} =$ {{1}}. Since the determinant is nonzero, the system has a {{2}} solution.
questions.explanation: The second column is $(0, 1, 0)^T$, so only the row-2 cofactor survives. The minor is $\begin{vmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 4 \end{vmatrix} = 8 - 1 = 7$, with cofactor sign $(+)$ at position (2,2). So $\det A = 7 \neq 0$, and the solution is unique.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics

questions.stem: A two-sector economy has $A = \begin{pmatrix} 0.2 & 0.2 \\ 0.4 & 0.2 \end{pmatrix}$ and final demand $d = \begin{pmatrix} 12 \\ 8 \end{pmatrix}$. Find $\det(I - A)$ and both gross outputs.
questions.solution: Here $I - A = \begin{pmatrix} 0.8 & -0.2 \\ -0.4 & 0.8 \end{pmatrix}$ with $\det(I - A) = 0.64 - 0.08 =$ {{1}}. Solving, the output of sector 1 is $x_1 =$ {{2}} and of sector 2 is $x_2 =$ {{3}}.
questions.source: in the style of Chiang and Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics
